en.extract:
The Australian crake (Porzana fluminea), also known as Australian spotted crake, or spotted crake is a species of bird in the family Rallidae. It is the only species of Australian crake in the genus Porzana.


Taxonomy
The Australian crake was formally described in 1843 by the English ornithologist John Gould and given the binomial name Porzana fluminea. He specified the type locality as New South Wales. The specific epithet is from Latin flumineus meaning "riverine" (from flumen meaning "river"). The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The Australian crake measures 19–23 cm (7.5–9.1 in) in length, weighs 55–70 g (1.9–2.5 oz) and has a wingspan of 27–33 cm (11–13 in). It is similar in appearance and behaviour to Baillon's crake (Porzana pusilla), but the Australian crake is stockier and darker overall and lacks the distinctive barred undertail of the Baillon's crake (Porzana pusilla). The Australian crake has a sooty face with steel-blue/dark grey breast, belly and throat. Brown upperparts, including the crown, are streaked black and white, with barred black and white flanks. Shorter undertail coverts are black and longer undertail coverts are white, forming a distinctive upside down V when tail is cocked. The outermost primaries have a distinctly white leading edge that is visible during flight. The bill is yellow-green with red base to upper mandible and measures 1.8–2.1 cm (0.71–0.83 in). Legs and feet are also yellow-green and the iris is red. While Gould asserted in The Birds of Australia (Gould) that "the sexes present so little difference in colour, that they are only to be distinguished by dissection", it can be noted that the female is slightly smaller and paler than male with a brown stripe across upper lores and more defined white spots on breast and neck. Immature birds are paler again with white fringed plumage on belly and breast appearing like muted barring. Juveniles are similar to adults but lack the steel-blue/dark grey plumage and instead have brown and white speckled underparts, as well as a brown iris and no red on bill. Chicks have very plumulaceous black feathers with a deep green hue and a distinctive, red blaze to base of upper mandible.


Distribution and habitat

Endemic to Australia, the Australian crake can be found throughout southeast and Western Australia. It is less common in Tasmania and tropical areas of northern Australia. The Australian crake lives in both coastal and inland habitats in freshwater, brackish, marine and terrestrial environments where it prefers densely vegetated areas of marshes, swamps, estuaries and saltmarshes that support lignum, chenopods, rushes and sedges. Distribution and movement is dependent on water conditions, so following periods of high rainfall, range can extend inland to areas like Alice Springs/Mparntwe.


Behaviour and ecology


Food and feeding
Foraging often occurs in pairs, family groups and sometimes, when food is abundant, in large groups of up to 100 individuals. Densely vegetated areas amongst reeds, on mudflats or in shallow water (below 5 cm in depth) are favoured, where a variety of foods are enjoyed including; aquatic plants, algae, seeds, molluscs, crustaceans, spiders, tadpoles and insects, both adult and larval, in orders including Dermaptera, Orthoptera, Coleoptera, Diptera, Lepidoptera and Hymenoptera. These birds forage by probing the ground, wading and swimming, submerging their heads underwater and knocking larger food items against the ground by the water's edge.


Breeding
Breeding occurs between August–February. Nests are often over or beside water 2–50 cm (0.79–19.69 in) above waterline within reeds, rushes, grasses and low shrubs. Additional nesting materials of rushes or grasses are laid over the nest in an inverted dome shape, and foliage is often flattened in an approach-ramp or stage leading up to the nest. Nests of individual mating pairs are often found together, with as many as 30 individual nests in a group. Clutches are of 3-6 eggs that are pale brown with dark brown, red-brown and black spots. The eggs are incubated by both parents. The young are precocial and nidifugous. Both sexes accompany the young.


Conservation
Although the Australian crake is listed as Least Concern, habitat loss, invasive and feral animals, agriculture and livestock grazing and climate change are threats to this species and the conservation of wetlands is fundamental to the survival of this species.

The '''Australian crake (''Porzana fluminea'')''', also known as '''Australian spotted crake''', or '''spotted crake''' is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Rallidae]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Australian Spotted Crake - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/species/auscra1 |access-date=2022-06-18 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> It is the only species of Australian crake in the genus ''[[Porzana]]''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Pizzey |first=Graham |url=https://www.vgls.vic.gov.au/client/en_AU/VGLS-public/search/detailnonmodal/ent:$002f$002fSD_ILS$002f0$002fSD_ILS:25863/ada?qu=Birds+--+Australia+--+Identification.&d=ent%3A%2F%2FSD_ILS%2F0%2FSD_ILS%3A25863%7EILS%7E30&ic=true&ps=300&h=8 |title=The field guide to the birds of Australia |publisher=Collins |year=1980 |isbn=978-0-7322-9193-8 |location=Sydney |oclc=505187991}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Finfoots, flufftails, rails, trumpeters, cranes, Limpkin – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flufftails/ |access-date=2023-07-07 |website=www.worldbirdnames.org}}</ref>
[[File:Australian crake (far right).png|thumb|Australian crake (far right). 1891 lithograph by Gracius Broinowski.<ref>G, Broinowski. 1891.The Birds of Australia, Tabuan Water Crake Or Sooty Crake, (colour litho). https://www-bridgemaneducation-com</ref>]]

==Taxonomy==
The Australian crake was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1843 by the English ornithologist [[John Gould]] and given the [[binomial name]] ''Porzana fluminea''. He specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as [[New South Wales]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1842 | title=''Porzana fluminea'' | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=10 | issue=117 | page=139 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30679901 }} Although bearing the year 1842 on the title page, the article did not appear until 1843.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1934 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=2 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=184 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482997 }}</ref> The specific epithet is from [[Latin]] ''flumineus'' meaning "riverine" (from ''flumen'' meaning "river").<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=162 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n162/mode/1up }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=Flufftails, finfoots, rails, trumpeters, cranes, limpkin | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flufftails/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=29 December 2023}}</ref>

==Description==
The Australian crake measures {{convert|19|-|23|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length, weighs {{convert|55|-|70|g||abbr=on}} and has a wingspan of {{convert|27|-|33|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":5">{{Cite book |first=Barry |last=Taylor |title=Rails: a guide to the rails, crakes, gallinules and coots of the world |date=30 August 2010 |publisher=A&C Black |isbn=978-1-4081-3537-2 |oclc=1053858279}}</ref><ref name=":6" /> It is similar in appearance and behaviour to Baillon's crake (''[[Baillon's crake|Porzana pusilla]]''), but the Australian crake is stockier and darker overall and lacks the distinctive barred undertail of the Baillon's crake (''[[Baillon's crake|Porzana pusilla]]'').<ref name=":6">{{Cite book |author=Campbell, Iain |title=Birds of australia: a photographic guide |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1-4008-6510-9 |oclc=884015045}}</ref> The Australian crake has a sooty face with steel-blue/dark grey breast, belly and throat. Brown upperparts, including the crown, are streaked black and white, with barred black and white flanks.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Shorter undertail coverts are black and longer undertail coverts are white, forming a distinctive upside down V when tail is cocked.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |author=Menkhorst, Peter |title=The Australian bird guide |date=August 2019 |publisher=CSIRO |isbn=978-1-4863-1193-4 |oclc=1096383391}}</ref> The outermost primaries have a distinctly white leading edge that is visible during flight.<ref name=":5" /> The bill is yellow-green with red base to upper mandible and measures {{convert|1.8|-|2.1|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=George Adams |title=Complete guide to Australian birds |date=2018 |isbn=978-0-14-378708-2 |location=North Sydney, NSW | publisher=Penguin |oclc=1046086429}}</ref> Legs and feet are also yellow-green and the iris is red.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /> While [[John Gould|Gould]] asserted in [[The Birds of Australia (Gould)]] that "the sexes present so little difference in colour, that they are only to be distinguished by dissection",<ref>{{Cite web |title=Volume VI |url=https://nla.gov.au/nla.obj-52986943 |access-date=2022-06-20 |website=Trove |language=en}}</ref> it can be noted that the female is slightly smaller and paler than male with a brown stripe across upper lores and more defined white spots on breast and neck.<ref name=":5" /> Immature birds are paler again with white fringed plumage on belly and breast appearing like muted barring.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":1" /> Juveniles are similar to adults but lack the steel-blue/dark grey plumage and instead have brown and white speckled underparts, as well as a brown iris and no red on bill. Chicks have very plumulaceous black feathers with a deep green hue and a distinctive, red blaze to base of upper mandible.<ref name=":2" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Australian crake distribution.png|thumb|Australian crake distribution.<ref>{{cite web | title=Atlas of Living Australia website | url=https://bie.ala.org.au/species/urn:lsid:biodiversity.org.au:afd.taxon:9a82127d-8ffa-4aa7-bcb6-e1ef527cb67d#gallery | access-date=17 June 2022}}</ref>]]
[[Endemic]] to [[Australia]], the Australian crake can be found throughout southeast and Western Australia. It is less common in Tasmania and tropical areas of northern Australia.<ref name=":1" /> The Australian crake lives in both coastal and inland habitats in freshwater, brackish, marine and terrestrial environments where it prefers densely vegetated areas of marshes, swamps, estuaries and saltmarshes that support [[Duma florulenta|lignum]], chenopods, rushes and sedges.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Australian Crake (Porzana fluminea) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/australian-crake-porzana-fluminea?gclid=CjwKCAjw77WVBhBuEiwAJ-YoJAB6Szkn1C9P5z1LcuIMlKN6S5obhtDrnFiFXOd7xoapBkixIBBZnBoCRCcQAvD_BwE |access-date=2022-06-18 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":2" /> Distribution and movement is dependent on water conditions, so following periods of high rainfall, range can extend inland to areas like [[Alice Springs|Alice Springs/''Mparntwe'']].<ref name=":1" />

==Behaviour and ecology==

=== Food and feeding ===
Foraging often occurs in pairs, family groups and sometimes, when food is abundant, in large groups of up to 100 individuals.<ref name=":5" /> Densely vegetated areas amongst reeds, on mudflats or in shallow water (below 5 cm in depth) are favoured, where a variety of foods are enjoyed including; aquatic plants, algae, seeds, molluscs, crustaceans, spiders, tadpoles and insects, both adult and larval, in orders including [[Dermaptera]], [[Orthoptera]], [[Beetle|Coleoptera]], [[Diptera]], [[Lepidoptera]] and [[Hymenoptera]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Australian Spotted Crake {{!}} BIRDS in BACKYARDS |url=https://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Porzana-fluminea#:~:text=The%20adult%20Australian%20Spotted%20Crake,of%20its%20tail%20is%20white. |access-date=2022-06-19 |website=www.birdsinbackyards.net}}</ref><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5" /> These birds forage by probing the ground, wading and swimming, submerging their heads underwater and knocking larger food items against the ground by the water's edge.<ref name=":5" />

=== Breeding ===
Breeding occurs between August–February.<ref name=":3" /> Nests are often over or beside water {{convert|2|-|50|cm|in|abbr=on}} above waterline within reeds, rushes, grasses and low shrubs. Additional nesting materials of rushes or grasses are laid over the nest in an inverted dome shape, and foliage is often flattened in an approach-ramp or stage leading up to the nest.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":3" /> Nests of individual mating pairs are often found together, with as many as 30 individual nests in a group.<ref name=":5" /> Clutches are of 3-6 eggs that are pale brown with dark brown, red-brown and black spots. The eggs are incubated by both parents. The young are [[precocial]] and [[nidifugous]]. Both sexes accompany the young.<ref name=hanzab>{{cite book | editor1-last=Marchant | editor1-first=S. | editor2-last=Higgins | editor2-first=P.J. |year=1993 | chapter=''Porzana fiuminea'' Australian crake | title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand & Antarctic Birds | volume=2, Raptors to lapwings | location=Melbourne, Victoria | publisher=Oxford University Press | isbn=978-0-19-553069-8 | pages=551–557, Plate 44 | chapter-url=https://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/sites/all/files/176_Australian%20Crake.pdf }}</ref>

== Conservation ==
Although the Australian crake is listed as Least Concern, habitat loss, invasive and feral animals, agriculture and livestock grazing and climate change are threats to this species and the conservation of wetlands is fundamental to the survival of this species.<ref>{{Cite web |last=AWE |date= |title=Wise Use Wetlands Factsheet |url=https://www.awe.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/wise-use-wetlands-factsheet.pdf |access-date=17 June 2022 |website=AWE}}</ref>
